writings.title: Bring What You Have and Watch God Multiply
writings.scriptureText: You give them something to eat, he responded.
writings.content:
This is Jesus's reply to the disciples when they mention that the crowd who had been listening to him needed food. The disciples' suggestion was to send the crowd away, so that the crowd could feed themselves. Jesus, probably knowing what miracle He was about to perform, first instructs the disciples to give the crowd something to eat. Why? Could Jesus not have just performed the miracle right then and there? Certainly He could have. However, if Jesus had performed the miracle on the spot, the same lesson would not have been taught to the disciples. Here is what I believe was going on in Jesus's head to respond in this way:

**1. Jesus wanted to emphasize that we *cannot* do this life on our own.** Sometimes, we need to stop and realize how little we ourselves really have to provide. All that we have comes from God. When Jesus responded in this way, let's take a look at the disciples' response:

> "They said to Him, 'Should we go and buy two hundred denarii worth of bread and give them something to eat?'" — Mark 6:37

While it is obviously hard to tell the tone of how the disciples replied, I believed that this was a reply trying to get Jesus to realize how *absurd* His response was. A denarii was one day's wage. The disciples are thus saying, you really think we can go and afford food worth 200 *days* of work for the people? Come on man, that's *absurd.* Jesus was teaching the disciples that what is impossible for man is possible for God (Matthew 19:16). What we cannot provide, God can.

**2. Once we realize how absurd/desperate the situation really is, and that we ourselves cannot deal with it, Jesus then teaches us to look at what we have.** After the disciples pointed out how absurd it would be for them to provide food for everyone, Jesus responds in this way:

> "He asked them, 'How many loaves do you have? Go and see.'" — Mark 6:38

We need to remember what we are going into the situation with. Jesus asks the disciples how many loaves they had so that they could see what He was about to do. If we fail to recognize what we have before we come to God, we will have no idea what He has done for us after He has provided. Jesus was teaching the disciples that we need to know and remember where we were before we come to God with a problem, so that we can know and appreciate where God takes us.

I believe the disciples also did not learn this lesson, at least not right away:

> "Then He got into the boat with them, and the wind ceased. They were completely astounded, because they had not understood about the loaves. Instead, their hearts were hardened." — Mark 6:51-52

> "Aware of this, He said to them, 'Why are you discussing the fact you have no bread? Don't you understand or comprehend? Do you have hardened hearts? Do you have eyes and not see; do you have ears and not hear? And do you not remember?'" — Mark 8:17-18

Even having witnessed this miracle first-hand, it was still in the disciples' nature to question Jesus and God. To rely on their own understanding and not God's. To look to themselves and not to Christ. Our hearts are easily hardened — we need to remember to allow God to soften them. Like the disciples, we see God do the miraculous, and yet we will still doubt. We all need to remember to ask God to not let our hearts harden to His wondrous testimonies.

**3. The last teaching that I see Jesus have here is God can take what we have and multiply it for His purposes.** This one is clearly seen in the story. The people have five loaves of bread and two fish. They bring them to Jesus, who brings it to God, who then provides enough to satisfy everyone's hunger and have more remaining (twelve baskets full of bread and fish). Let us not forget that it is God who multiplies.

Another beautiful thing about this part of the story is how God uses the offering of the *few* to satisfy the *many*. The disciples went around to all the people and found five loaves of bread and two fish. People offered what they had — while it was enough for themselves and their family, it was not enough for the entire crowd. Instead of being greedy and hoarding it for themselves though, they were willing to give it up to share with others. And what did they lose? Absolutely nothing. Instead, it was only gain — there was more bread at the end than the beginning and *everyone* was satisfied. These people offered what they had and God did not let them down. When we give God all that we have, even though it might be practically nothing (especially for the situation), we will see Him take care of others *and* ourselves. We can trust Him. We just have to be willing to give him all of our bread.

writings.title: Simply Thankful
writings.content:
A beautiful verse. So simple and plain, but one that we often forget to practice. We come to God with our wants and our needs, but do we come with our gratitude? Hardly, if ever! Why is it so hard? All we do is want. It is when the gospel is least on our minds that our wants and desires are at their most high. I am not saying that want is necessarily a bad thing — God has given us desires that are good to pursue. We should *want* to want to spend time with God. That is a good desire. To be happy is a good desire, so long as we are happy and content with and in God. But oh how we can fall into the illusion that we need this, that, or the other thing to be happy. Rarely, do I stop and think about what God has done for me. Yes, I can thank God for certain things in my life daily: my wife mainly and now our little daughter, the food that I eat, that I'm alive and woke up. But is my heart truly grateful to God or am I just doing these things out of dead religion? I think it is hard to have a heart that is genuinely grateful to God, when we are just thanking Him for the same things over and over. That is not to say that I should not thank God for these things daily, but when that is all I thank Him for, I find the gratitude is not there as it *ought* to be. I have been given far too much to not be grateful. I have been given the precious blood of Jesus Christ. With this alone, I should be the most grateful human being ever. All the rest of God's blessings will be seen as just that — *blessings*. The truth of the matter is this — I have lived a life worthy of damnation, not heaven. We all have: > "For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God." — Romans 3:23 > "The wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord." — Romans 6:23 It is easy to despair when reading Romans 3:23. We have fallen short of the glory of God, the Creator of heaven and earth. He created us with intention, purpose, and worth and we have completely degraded it in ourselves and others. We have taken this valuable gift of life and told God that we know how to live it better than He could ever have designed us. Today, I listened to a sermon by C.H. Spurgeon titled, *"In Him: Like Him"*, where he said: > "Sin promised freedom, but brought us bondage." — C.H. Spurgeon Our sin promised us freedom — to live a life unconstrained from the design of God. Surely we should do what deep down we feel we want to do. If it goes against the nature of God, so be it. I want to do it and so I shall. I know better than God. And our reward? Bondage — we will become so enslaved to our sin we will find that we cannot get out of it. The wages of our sin is death. But wait, we have been given a gift! In Christ Jesus our Lord — eternal life! How can we as Christians not be *the most grateful people?* We take our eyes off of the cross and put the focus back on ourselves. It is necessary to die to ourselves and our own will. And painful. Oh so painful is the process of sanctification. For once we have been born again in Christ, we are at war. There is a war continually going on inside us between the old self and the new self. We know the ways of God, but we fall short time and time again on our own pursuit of holiness. The Apostle Paul said it best: > "For I do not do the good that I want to do, but I practice the evil that I do not want to do… So I discover this law: When I want to do what is good, evil is present with me. For in my inner self I delight in God's law, but I see a different law in the parts of my body, waging war against the law of my mind and taking me prisoner to the law of sin in the parts of my body. What a wretched man I am! Who will rescue me from this body of death? Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord! So then, with my mind I myself am serving the law of God, but with my flesh, the law of sin." — Romans 7:19, 21-25 Paul found a new kind of law, such that when he tried to do good, sin sprang up in him. The good he *wanted* to do, he didn't do. But the evil he *did not want* to do he kept on doing. As Spurgeon said, sin promised freedom but actually only brought about bondage. When we give our lives to Christ we are changed. No longer do we want to practice our evil ways but follow the good ways of the LORD. But we cannot do this without God. He will rescue us through Christ Jesus. He will sanctify us. He has given us eternal life. And for this, we should be thankful! When we remember our old condition, the bondage we lived in to sin, and the rescue that Jesus Christ brought about, we should have all the gratitude we need. The other blessings God brings about in our lives: family, food, shelter, friends, etc. will become all the more sweet. More importantly, we will find that our wants change — both in what we want to pursue, and the abundance of things we want. Godliness with contentment is great gain (1 Timothy 6:6) — such that we will want godliness and be content with it. I encourage you all to remember the gratitude we should have to God through the death and resurrection of Christ for our sins. Give God the glory and give Him this gratitude — the gratitude He deserves. And be thankful. For the little things, the big things, and all the things in between.
For when I see what He has done for me,
How could my heart not sing with glee?

Wretched man that I was and still am,
God has saved me with His unblemished lamb.

Now I can come before Him with thanksgiving,
And eternal life shall I start living.

writings.title: His Comfort for Our Joy
writings.scriptureText: When I am filled with cares, Your comfort brings me joy.
writings.content:
What a beautiful verse that we should always have at the forefront of our minds. We are filled with cares and God’s comfort brings us joy. But what cares was David talking about in this Psalm? The Hebrew word that cares is translated from is śar‘appîm (שַׂרְעַפִּים) which refers to disquieting, anxious, or divided thoughts that disturb the mind, often reflecting a multitude of internal worries. So, from this verse, our cares are those thoughts that disturb our mind. If we look further into the verse, we see that it has the pattern: when I, then God. When *I* am filled with cares then *God’s* comforts bring me joy. What are we looking to when our thoughts are divided and our mind is disturbed? Do we look at ourselves, the world, or anything that is not God to try to be comforted? I would assume for all of us, the answer is yes (at least a lot more of the time than it should be). Well, what are God’s comforts? Or, how does God’s comfort bring joy? God’s comfort lies in His word and in His character. When we are filled with cares, I challenge you to spend time meditating on God’s character and on His true Word. What care are you filled with today? Perhaps you are worried about your soul\! The enemy throws thought after thought after thought at you about your past. Remember God’s word: >“But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God.” *\- 1 Corinthians 6:11* This verse was written by the Apostle Paul, but it is exactly what is needed when the enemy tempts us to doubt our salvation due to the past. It even seems in this case that Paul might be admonishing the Corinthians for practicing some unacceptable behavior after having been washed, sanctified, and justified. Sometimes, our thoughts get the best of us and they cause us to act in a way that is unacceptable for a Christian. We need to remind ourselves that we were washed, sanctified, and justified in Jesus’s name and by the Holy Spirit. Perhaps you say, “this is a good verse but it is not God’s word.” This is not entirely true. However, for the sake of argument, let us look to what the LORD said Himself for this same care I mentioned earlier: >“Do not fear, for I have redeemed you; I have called you by your name; you are mine.” \- Isaiah 43:1 Different verse, similar context. Do not fear your salvation. God has redeemed you. In fact, He called you *specifically* and you are His. When we really think, reflect, and meditate on this verse, how can we not find joy in the comfort of our God and what He has done for us? Perhaps you would argue further that these promises of old cannot be for you. Well, I can assure you that if you are in Christ this promise is for you: >“For every one of God’s promises is ‘Yes’ in him \[Jesus\].” \- 2 Corinthians 1:20 Notice that this does not say *some* of God’s promises but *every one* of God’s promises is “yes” in Jesus. This is wonderful\! Pick any time in the Bible that you see God make a promise. This promise has then been given to you through Jesus Christ. And this is the reason we *must* have much of God’s promises and truth built up within us. For, if we do not have some of God’s promises known, how will we combat our cares? I provided examples for only one scenario, however, we need to build up our arsenal of God’s truth to combat our cares. This is seen perfectly with the armor of God: >“Finally, be strengthened by the Lord and by his vast strength. Put on the full armor of God so that you can stand against the schemes of the devil. For our struggle is not against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the cosmic powers of this darkness, against evil, spiritual forces in the heavens. For this reason take up the full armor of God, so that you may be able to resist in the evil day, and having prepared everything, to take your stand. Stand, therefore, with truth like a belt around your waist, righteousness like armor on your chest, and your feet sandaled with readiness for the gospel of peace. In every situation take up the shield of faith with which you can extinguish all the flaming arrows of the evil one. Take the helmet of salvation and the sword of the Spirit—which is the word of God.” \- Ephesians 6:10-17 Why do we need the armor of God? So that we can stand against the schemes of the devil\! Notice that Paul does not tell us to put on *some* of the armor of God but the *full* armor of God. If we want to stand a chance we need truth, righteousness, the gospel of peace, faith, salvation, and the word of God. We *cannot* walk in this life expecting victory without these things. In my view, many of our cares are planted by the enemy. Especially doubts \- the devil will use whatever tricks he can to make you doubt your faith in God. We must stand firm then with the full armor of God. For with the full armor of God equipped, our cares will be replaced with joy. We turn away from ourselves and toward God whose faithful love endures forever (Psalm 136). I promise you (and I myself need to remember this promise more than most\!) that if we know, meditate, and reflect on God’s character, our cares will be turned into joy by Him.
The devil schemes
In my head with hateful screams

Such that I am filled with anxious care
Then I remember that you know my every hair

So I suit up everyday
And know the LORD will take my cares away

writings.title: A Time to Sow
writings.scriptureText: Sow righteousness for yourselves and reap faithful love; break up your unplowed ground. It is time to seek the LORD until He comes and sends righteousness on you like the rain.
writings.content:
We are to sow, so that we may reap. In our modern age, we get anything and everything right when we want it. Need some food? Order it on doordash. Want some clothes? Get them the next day with Amazon. Therefore, we have become disconnected with sowing and reaping spiritually. We are far too used to getting what we want when we want it. When we come before God and ask for the desires of our heart, we expect to get them right away. Even if they are good desires, God has a timing that is better than our own. We must learn to take time to sow, so that we may reap. There is a promise here though, *if you sow* righteousness *you will reap* faithful love. To do so, we need to break up our unplowed ground. Therefore, in order to sow seed for ourselves, we must first be plowed. This process may be painful, but it is necessary. We must be prepared to let go of what we must in order to humbly receive the Word of God. Sometimes, in the process, our sinful nature will be revealed as so much more sinful than we ever thought. And that is the point. We need to know how deprived of righteousness we are on our own, so that we can turn to the only one who can save us, Jesus Christ. How do we sow this righteousness for ourselves? We must seek the LORD so that He will come and send righteousness on us like the rain. The righteousness is poured onto us *from* Him. Let us not be deceived into believing that this righteousness is our own: >“All of us have become like something unclean, and all our righteous acts are like a polluted garment; all of us wither like a leaf, and our iniquities carry us away like the wind.” \- Isaiah 64:6 A rather humbling verse to read. All of our righteous acts are a polluted garment. When we strive for righteousness on our own, the corruption of our sin will pollute the act in some way. How can this be? Well, an example could be that we desire to do something good in hopes that this will in turn benefit ourselves more. Therefore, the good act is stained by our own selfish ambition. I’m sure there are many other examples that we could think of. The point is, when we strive on our own, our sinful nature will always trip us up. Therefore, we *must* seek the LORD and He *will* come and send righteousness on us. For remember the LORD and the Lord Jesus Christ, respectively said: >“You will seek me and find me when you search for me with all your heart.” \- Jeremiah 29:13 >“Ask, and it will be given to you. Seek, and you will find. Knock, and the door will be opened to you. For everyone who asks receives, and the one who seeks finds, and to the one who knocks, the door will be opened.” \- Matthew 7:7-8 These are two very important promises that we receive from the Father and the Son that should inspire us in our pursuit of sowing righteousness. Seek God with your whole heart and He will be found. For when we seek we will indeed find. What is important is that we need our whole heart dedicated to finding the LORD. We cannot be half-hearted in this. I see this in my own life, I want to seek the LORD with all that I am, but I often let various distractions grab my attention. Before I know it, the day is up and I am no closer to seeing God than I was when the day started. In fact, I may be further. I become frustrated that I don’t see God as I would have liked, but no wonder, when I seek with half my heart. C.S. Lewis said it best: >“It would seem that Our Lord finds our desires not too strong, but too weak. We are half-hearted creatures, fooling about with drink and sex and ambition when infinite joy is offered us, like an ignorant child who wants to go on making mud pies in a slum because he cannot imagine what is meant by the offer of a holiday at the sea. We are far too easily pleased.” \- C.S. Lewis (The Weight of Glory) This is true in my life. Infinite joy is offered to me and yet I would rather waste away with some worldly pleasure that leaves me broken and unsatisfied. I seek to be filled up with God’s creation and not by God Himself. I used to think that this meant my desires and passions (lust, greed, idolatry, etc.) were too strong, and they needed to be quenched with a lesser desire. But, I now find that my desires were actually too weak. My desire to be happy and whole was and is too weak, for if I wanted it truly, I would find it in the only true source, God Himself. I need to remember: >“In your presence is abundant joy; at your right hand are eternal pleasures.” \- Psalm 16:11 It is time to seek the LORD \- in His presence my joy will be abundant. This joy is not 99% joy, but greater than 100%. That is abundance – more than we can take. Additionally, my longing for pleasure will be satisfied as well in the presence of God with eternal pleasures. Things that I cannot even imagine. Oh, my soul, it is time to seek\! As I close and reflect, the main verse is a verse I have meditated on a bit, but as I return to it today, I realize that I always thought that sowing righteousness meant striving to do good acts. However, when I look back on Isaiah 64:6, I no longer think this is true. Sowing righteousness is about seeking the LORD. Seek Him through his word every day. Seek Him in His creation, remembering that He made the earth and all of its inhabitants. Seek union with Him through His Son. When we seek God, we will find Him. And as we come to know God, and sow His word, truth, and promises into ourselves, the Holy Spirit will pour righteousness into our lives. We will become like Christ (1 John 3:2). So come, let us seek the LORD with our *whole* heart and not waver in our pursuit, for we will find Him. And it will be glorious.
Oh my soul it is time to sow
For it is the LORD you wish to know

Let go of all you hold dear
So that to the LORD you may draw near

And when we think what we’ve lost will bring pain
We will see that with the LORD all is gain

writings.title: Out of Darkness, Into the Light
writings.scriptureText: “He will bring me into the light; I will see his salvation.”
writings.content:
This verse comes from Micah, who was a prophet in 8th and 7th century B.C., preaching the downfall of both Israel and Judah. In the verses previous to this, we can see the true moral decline of the people: >“Faithful people have vanished from the land; there is no one upright among the people. All of them wait in ambush to shed blood; they hunt each other with a net. Both hands are good at accomplishing evil: the official and the judge demand a bribe; when the powerful man communicates his evil desire, they plot it together.” \- Micah 7:2-3 People that were faithful to the LORD had completely vanished from both the Northern and Southern Kingdoms. There was no one left who was upright. People were plotting against one another and only looking after themselves. The moral decline is clearly seen. We read these words and we might be stirred to self righteousness. But, we have been no better. It is only when we live a life in union with Jesus Christ, that we put away our selfish ambition and live to serve others. The Apostle Paul said it better: >“I have been crucified with Christ, and I no longer live, but Christ lives in me. The life I now live in the body, I live by faith in the Son of God, who loved me and gave himself for me.” \- Galatians 2:20 What *glorious* words. Christ lived *in* Paul\! I want this. Nay, I need this, or my soul shall perish. Jesus loved me and gave Himself for me. When Christ lives within us, we will give ourselves for others. Notice that this is the opposite of what we see in Micah 7:2-3. With Christ living in us, our hands are no longer good at accomplishing evil, but they are good at doing the will of God. Let us not get on our high horse though. Look at the main text for today. It does not say *I* brought myself into the light. Nor does it say I will see *my* salvation. No, it is not so. God has brought us into the light, if we are in the light. God has brought us His salvation through Jesus Christ. What gifts of grace we have received to be granted these blessings\! Let us never forget to thank God for them. I cannot help but feel that we as Christians miss this when we think about the world though. We often thank God (although not as often as we should) for our salvation. If we truly believe that He brought us into the light and that it is His salvation, we must not walk about with self righteousness. No, we should walk about in humility, grateful that we have been given the blessing of eternal life. But having received this gift of eternal life we can often view ourselves above the world \- above those who are not yet saved. We look down on them in condemnation, as if what has been given to us by God was actually something we have been given ourselves. Let us not be deceived: >“Every good and perfect gift comes from above, coming down from the Father of lights, who does not change like the shifting shadows.” \- James 1:17 Like we discussed earlier and as we read here, every good and perfect gift (our faith included) is from above. It is not our work, but God’s work within us. I think this should completely change the way we look at people. Instead of condemnation we should pray for mercy. “God I beg you please be merciful to these people and open their eyes so that they may see, as you have allowed me to see.” The way we as Christians interact with the world is vital to the thriving of the gospel. How we go about sharing the gospel and our values matters \- people respond to various situations in different ways. Let us look to another verse written by the Apostle Paul: >“Remind them to submit to rulers and authorities, to obey, to be ready for every good work, to slander no one, to avoid fighting, and to be kind, always showing gentleness to all people. For we too were once foolish, disobedient, deceived, enslaved by various passions and pleasures, living in malice and envy, hateful, detesting one another.” \- Titus 3:1-3 This is Paul speaking to Titus to remind Christians how to interact with the world. The freedom we have gained in Christ is *not* an excuse to defy authority. Nor is it an excuse to be harsh and unkind to people. Notice that it is not talking about being harsh or unkind to Christians \- no, the text says *all* people. We watch TV as the world celebrates lust and sexual immorality and other various passions. In our self righteousness we condemn them \- we are harsh in our dealings with the world. Why is this? Our master has absolutely not dealt this harshly with us, so why do we do it to them? Now, I am not excusing their immoral behavior. It is immoral and they do need to repent. What I do think we need to work on as a Church body is how we go about telling the world of their sins. And that is, “to be kind, always showing gentleness to all people.” The temptation will be there, for we are prideful beings, to deal harshly and unkindly with people. There are certainly times where this is absolutely necessary. But there are also many times where we deal harshly and it damages the spread of the gospel. When we are struggling to be kind and gentle to the unregenerate around us let us remember the kindness the LORD has shown us. For, like the scripture says, we were once living in these same passions that we see people enslaved to. Let us remember the darkness we were once in. Not fondly, and with no wish to return. But let us remember the miserable wretches we once were without the light that God has revealed to us in Jesus Christ. Then remember the world. They are still living in this darkness from which God saved us. Let us learn to be more gentle and kind to those around us. It is not usually the preaching against one's actions that will save them. We need to remember that these people genuinely find no error in their way, for they are in the dark. So, preaching against their actions will only enrage them and push them farther from the gospel. What they need preached is simple \- the gospel. So we must preach to them this: >“For I passed on to you as most important what I also received: that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, that he was buried, that he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures.” \- 1 Corinthians 15:3-4 Preaching the gospel will save those who will be saved by God. Therefore, let us throw aside our harsh words and unkind actions towards the world and preach to them what they need \- the gospel of Jesus Christ. For, as the main text says, God will bring them into the light and they will see His salvation just as He has done for us. May the LORD grant us all hearts to be gentle, kind, and utterly relentless in spreading the gospel. Amen.
Once darkness was all I saw,
Such that I did not know my flaw.

Until one glorious day,
The gospel was preached my way.

The light I have seen it so,
And God’s salvation I do now know.

writings.title: My King Prays for Me
writings.scriptureText: ‘But I have prayed for you that your faith may not fail.’
writings.content:
These are Our Lord’s words to Peter right before He predicts that Peter will deny Him three times. What an amazing thing! Jesus prayed for Peter, that his faith would not fail. Notice that Jesus did not pray to take the cup away from Peter, but rather, Jesus prayed that this test would not destroy his faith. Instead, the goal of this test was to have Peter turn back to Jesus and strengthen his brothers in the faith: >‘And you, when you have turned back, strengthen your brothers.’ - Luke 22:32 So, often our trials arise so that we can turn back to God, our one and only Source so that our faith may be strengthened and we may strengthen the faith of others. While, at first glance, this might discourage us, I think this is one of the most encouraging things to know about our walk with Christ. From this passage, we know that our faith will be tested such that Jesus will have to pray that our faith will not fail. Things will come about in this life that make us question whether we really believe at all. Or at least it makes us question the goodness of God and His support of us. In these moments, it's hard to look to anything but despair, but we need despair not. Like Peter, Jesus has told us: >‘Simon, Simon, look out. Satan has asked to sift you like wheat.’ - Luke 22:31 Times are certain to come in our own lives when Satan will ask to sift us like wheat. He will do whatever he can to destroy our faith. Vile thoughts, wicked suggestions, physical ailments, loss of a loved one, disease, cancer, you name it. Although it always surprises us and even is detrimental to our faith it should not be so. When we are in a season of extreme testing, we need not question our faith (even though inevitably we all will). Instead, we can cling to this story. Satan has asked to sift us like wheat, but Jesus has prayed for us so that our faith will not fail and that we may strengthen the faith of others. In the moment, it is hard to look past whatever circumstances we are experiencing. However, if we look toward the bigger picture we can rest knowing our King prays for us. Let us not despair these times when they come, but rejoice that we have a Savior willing to pray for us! Now, you might say, this is all well and good, but Jesus here is praying for Peter not me. Ahh, okay, well let us look some other places in the scriptures: >Therefore, he is able to save completely those who come to God through him, since he always lives to intercede for them. - Hebrews 7:25 >Who is the one who condemns? Christ Jesus is the one who died, but even more, has been raised; he also is at the right hand of God and intercedes for us. - Romans 8:34 It is not just Peter who Christ would pray for, but Christ lives and intercedes for all of us at the right hand of God. Jesus Christ, King of Kings, Lord of Lords, Prince of Peace, prays for you to God. This is wonderful news! When we sit and meditate on this fact, it is hard to imagine anything else but peace flowing into our hearts, knowing that Jesus is praying for us. There’s still more comfort to be found here. Jesus did not pray for Peter after Peter denied Him three times, but before Peter denied Him three times. God knows our problems before they happen and Christ has already prayed that our faith will not be shaken. We can trust God to provide what we need, when we need it to get us through our darkest times. When all we see is darkness, God sees through it: >If I say, ‘Surely the darkness will hide me, and the light around me will be night’— even the darkness is not dark to you. The night shines like the day; darkness and light are alike to you. - Psalm 139:11-12 No darkness within or around us is too dark for God to handle. He knows what darkness will be around and within us. Remember, Jesus Christ has already prayed for us that our faith may not fail. Hold fast to this. Hold fast to the fact that darkness and light are alike to God. Nothing gets past Him and He will use this darkness for His glory. We also must recognize one last thing: Jesus told Peter that when he turns back he should strengthen his brothers. This means that Peter would turn away from the Lord (which we see), but that he was able to come back to the Lord. Have you strayed too deep? Have you let sin or worry or fear control your life? Is darkness all you see such that it is dark, dark, and darker still? Did you lean on your pride and yourself to save you? Turn back! Turn back to the Lord Jesus Christ, who died for you on that cross. Darkness and light are alike to Him. He will rescue you. Always. For He prayed for you, and the Father will answer His prayers. And when Christ rescues you, remember to share, so that your brothers may be strengthened in the faith.
What great love is this?
My King showers me in prayers of bliss.

Oh, how I ran astray.
But, Jesus washed my sins away!

And my faith is secure.
For my Savior prayed for cure.

writings.title: Believe You are Clean
writings.scriptureText: ‘My father, if the prophet had told you to do some great thing, would you not have done it? How much more should you do it when he only tells you wash and be clean?’
writings.content:
Wash and be clean. This is something you would expect to find in the New Testament, but the verse today is actually from the Old Testament. To provide some quick context, Naaman, a pagan army commander in ancient times, has a troubling skin disease. He is made aware of Elisha, a prophet of the Lord GOD, and his ability to cure the disease. So, as would anyone, he sets out to meet Elisha and be healed. When Naaman comes to Elisha, he is simply told: 

>‘Go wash seven times in the Jordan and your skin will be restored and you will be clean.’ - 2 Kings 5:10

What a simple task! Go, wash yourself in a river seven times and be clean. It sounds too good to be true. In fact, it is suspiciously easy for such a serious problem. When Naaman hears this instruction he is outraged. He thinks the same thing that I have just written - Elisha must be messing with him. There is no way that the solution to this grand disease can simply be, “wash and be clean”. This is what Naaman was expecting instead:

>‘I was telling myself: He will surely come out, stand and call on the name of the Lord his God, and wave his hand over the place and cure the skin disease.’ - 2 Kings 5:11

Naaman was expecting something more mystical to happen for his disease to be healed, but instead he was given something simple and practical. His expectations of how the problem would be fixed did not line up with Elisha’s simple instructions. Therefore, Naaman was going to leave and not even attempt what Elisha said. That’s where the verse of the day comes in. One of his servants approaches him and says what we read in 2 Kings 5:13. The servant makes an excellent observation - if Elisha had asked Naaman to do something great, he would have done it. However, because the request was so simple Naaman refused to do it out of pride. He relied on his own understanding of how to fix this problem (i.e. something grand needed to occur) and not on Elisha’s. 

There are two major connections I can make from this story to God & Jesus. First, in what I just said, Naaman trusted and relied on his understanding and not Elisha’s. This often mirrors our own relationship with God. In proverbs we read:

>Trust in the LORD with all your heart, and do not rely on your own understanding; in all your ways know Him, and He will make your paths straight. -Proverbs 3:5-6

We are instructed to trust in God and not ourselves. Not a half-hearted trust, mind you. But 100% trust in Him with our whole heart. When we rely on our own understanding, our own feelings, and our own thoughts we will certainly fall into trouble. We must remember that the human heart is deceptive and will lead us astray. Therefore, we need to learn to let go of our pride, rid ourselves of our own understanding, and trust in the LORD. This is exactly what Naaman needed here. His pride almost cost him the cure to his disease. Had his servant not stepped in and suggested that he follow Elisha’s instructions, he would have never found the healing he so desperately sought. It makes me wonder, in what areas of our own lives are we letting pride block out the simple healing of God?

The next connection I make is to the sacrifice of Christ Himself, and the implications this has to all. This has to do with the actual instructions Naaman was given, “wash and be clean.” This makes me think of the following verse from the book of Romans:

>If you confess with your mouth, ‘Jesus is Lord,’ and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved. - Romans 10:9

Like the instructions Elisha gave Naaman, these are rather simple. Confess that Christ is Lord, and believe that God raised Him for the dead and you will be saved. You see, oftentimes we can make it so complicated. Like Naaman, we are expecting a much bigger production for our salvation. We are told, simply acknowledge that Jesus Christ is your Lord and believe God raised Him from the dead and you will be saved. It ends there. There is no striving, no proving, just plain and simple salvation.

Now, this verse could definitely be taken out of context or could be misconstrued. If we confess that Jesus is Lord and really do believe God raised Him from the dead, how should this change our lives? If Jesus Christ really and truly is your Lord, would you obey Him to the very end? If He were to come to you and command you to sell all your possessions to give to the poor would you? What if he told you to get up, move, and share the gospel in a completely uncomfortable place for you? Or how about this, what if Jesus Christ came and told you to give up your career to go into ministry? Would you obey? These are some questions we must all ask ourselves. And if Jesus Christ is our Lord, the answer would always be yes.

There are other areas of our lives in which we must acknowledge Jesus Christ is Lord. Perhaps it is our sexuality. God had created us male and female, such that one man and one woman are bound together for life in an exclusive sexual partnership. This is God’s design of sexuality - there is nothing else. And if God ordained it, Jesus is Lord over it. So, would you obey Jesus with your sexuality? Would you give up your lust for women to be with the one woman God has for you? Jesus said himself, that whoever lusts after a woman in his heart has committed adultery with her. Or would you give up whatever other sexually immoral behavior you have been tempted with? If Jesus Christ is Lord, the answer must be yes. There are many other things too, outside of sex - this was just an example. Lust, greed, idolatry, anger, rage, etc. are all others which the Bible explicitly warns us against. 

Jesus said it best himself:

>‘Why do you call me ‘Lord, Lord,’ and don’t do the things I say?’ - Luke 6:46

While we read earlier that we must confess Jesus as Lord, Jesus takes this a step further. If we are to call Jesus Christ our Lord and Savior, we must obey what He says. We cannot call Him Lord and disobey. We cannot believe that Jesus is our Savior, without believing the other things He has instructed us to do. In other words, we cannot pick and choose what parts of the Bible we wish to believe and obey - we must obey it all. Where is Jesus calling you to obey?

I do not wish to seem harsh when I say these words, but rather call us to remember how worthy Jesus is to be served. There is far more love, joy, and peace following Him than following the world. Nothing should outweigh our pursuit of Jesus. No relationship, no item, nothing. However, we are sinners and in need of grace, even after confessing Jesus is Lord and believing God raised Him from the dead. For while we have been justified we are still being sanctified. And eventually we will be glorified when we rise and are like Jesus himself. 

Do not let the enemy take your salvation from you. He will play trick after trick after trick to get you to walk away from God. To walk away from Jesus Christ. The enemy wants us to believe that the sin we continue to commit throughout our walk with God has stripped us of our salvation. Let me assure you, that if you struggle with this, I do too. And I’m sure every brother and sister in Christ has too. However, we need not fear. First, if we recognize and hate our sin, this to me (and to many others whom I have read) is a good indicator of our salvation. Once we accept Jesus Christ, we have been born again, and our new form cannot delight in sin. For while we will sadly always sin in this world, even after being saved, we will forever hate and work to eradicate our sin. Second, we must remember the words of the Apostle Paul:

>But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God. - 1 Corinthians 6:11

Does this ring a bell? Like Elisha had told Naaman, “wash and be clean”, we too must remember that we were washed, sanctified, justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Holy Spirit. Nothing the devil can do can take away our salvation. He can work to make us believe that he can, but remember, and repeat to yourself that you were washed, sanctified, and justified in the name above all names, Jesus Christ!

writings.title: Embrace the Night
writings.scriptureText: I long for you in the night
writings.content:
My writing today will sadly be short and sweet, for it is late and I am tired. But, today, I listened to a sermon titled, “The Desire of the Soul in Spiritual Darkness”, by Charles H. Spurgeon and I thought I could share a quick writing based on what I listened to. 

Here, in this verse, Spurgeon is looking at the symbolism of night. We long for God in the night, or in our spiritual darkness. It is often when we cannot see, feel, experience, or think about God as we believe we ought to, that we long for God the most. In these moments, Spurgeon warns us that Satan will try to convince us that we were never saved. He says: 

>There was a season when I flattered myself that I knew something about godliness and God; but now I doubt whether I have any part or lot in the matter. Satan suggests that I must dwell in endless flames. - C.H. Spurgeon

We are feeling spiritually alive and well, and then one day we doubt whether we really ever had part in the Kingdom of God at all. Did we ever really love Christ? Did we ever receive grace? Perhaps we have learned that our soul is more corrupt than we ever thought possible. Your mind has imagined awful and corrupt things that it never had before you were supposedly saved. Why now does it imagine and dwell on such horrible things? How could we ever be saved with such moral filth? Why must our souls be so downcast? We cry out like David:

>Why, my soul, are you so dejected? Why are you in such turmoil? - Psalm 43:5

No longer are we living in the day, but we must face the night. Scary thoughts surround us, and all we can do is long for God in the night. Let me ask, does Satan suggest you must dwell in eternal flames in the night? Do you believe him? We all admittedly do at some points. But let me ask you this, do you long for God in the night like never before? Not for what God can give you but for God Himself? I’m sure you do, and through Jesus Christ you will find Him. Our longing for God in the night is a sure reminder of our salvation.

Why does God not come to our rescue immediately in the night? Why does He not provide the redemption we so crave and plead for? The darkness of sin becomes darker yet and we know our one salvation is through Christ Jesus our Lord. Yet, when we cry out for help, we seem to be no closer to deliverance. Why? Spurgeon says this:

>He wants thee to see more of the blackness of darkness and of the horrible pit of sin, that like a burnt child thou mayest shun the fire for ever. He lets thee go down into the dungeon, that thou mayest prize liberty the better when it comes. And he is keeping thee waiting, moreover, that thy longings may be quickened. He knows that delay will fan the desire, and that if he keeps you waiting it will not be a loss to you, but will gain you much, because you will see your necessity more clearly, seek him more earnestly, cry more bitterly and your heart will be more in earnest after him. - C.H. Spurgeon

There is great benefit in waiting. Even when we cannot see it, we must remember that God is Sovereign and He can. It makes me think of the following passage from Deuteronomy:

>Be careful that you don’t forget the Lord your God by failing to keep his commands, ordinances, and statutes that I am giving you today. When you eat and are full, and build beautiful houses to live in, and your herds and flocks grow large, and your silver and gold multiply, and everything else you have increases, be careful that your heart doesn’t become proud and you forget the Lord your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the place of slavery. He led you through the great and terrible wilderness with its poisonous snakes and scorpions, a thirsty land where there was no water. He brought water out of the flint rock for you. He fed you in the wilderness with manna, which your ancestors had not known, in order to humble and test you, so that in the end he might cause you to prosper. You may say to yourself, ‘My power and my own ability have gained this wealth for me,’ but remember that the Lord your God gives you the power to gain wealth, in order to confirm his covenant he swore to your ancestors, as it is today. - Deuteronomy 8:11-18

When we are secure in the day, we often become complacent. We believe that the prosperity in our lives, even if that prosperity is spiritual and not economical, comes from our own doing or righteousness. In the day, we can so easily forget the one who brought about the day. And if God brought us out of darkness into light in our timing, our pride would most likely lead us to believe it was our own doing and not His answering of our prayers. 

You see, it is in the night that we learn to depend on God - not in the day. And while I have talked about the night being our realization of sin, I believe this can be any time of spiritual darkness or depression. But let me ask you this - if Christ were only in the night, would you follow? The answer should always be a resounding yes! 

Let us learn to embrace the night. Not in terms of giving into our darkness, but in seeking our God. The book of James put it perfectly:

>Consider it a great joy, my brothers and sisters, whenever you experience various trials, because you know that the testing of your faith produces endurance. And let endurance have its full effect, so that you may be mature and complete, lacking nothing. - James 1:2-4

We are to face the trial of night with great joy! For when the trial has passed our faith will be deeper and we will be more mature and complete in our Lord Jesus Christ. And if we think back to the words of David earlier, we can glean what we should do in our spiritual depression by learning what he did: 

>Why, my soul, are you so dejected? Why are you in such turmoil? Put your hope in God, for I will still praise him, my Savior and my God. - Psalm 43:5

Like we see in Isaiah, we are to long for God in the night. And from David, when our souls are depressed, remember to put your hope in God and praise Him still. No matter when or how He rescues you (even if it takes till heaven) Jesus Christ is worthy of all of our praise. So, brothers and sisters, let us learn to embrace the night, since it is in the night that we long for our God. God, come and rescue us from the night. Let us see, know, and savor You for who You are. And let us never forget You in the day, but rejoice all the more. For Christ’s sake, Amen.

writings.title: Easter Daily Devotional #1
writings.scriptureText: For I passed on to you as most important what I also received: that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, that He was buried, that He was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures.
writings.content:
This is perhaps the most plain statement of the gospel in all of the Bible. Not plain as in, “dull/uninteresting” but plain as in clear. What is the gospel? It’s that, “Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures”. Fantastic news! We have all sinned and fallen short of the glory of God. Jesus Christ came to forgive us of our sins. To break us free from the power of sin over us, so that we could have a righteous relationship with God.

But, there is more! Jesus was raised on the third day. Not only did Jesus die for our sins, but He rose again. And since He rose again, so shall we. 

>When Christ who is your life appears, then you also will appear with Him in glory. - Colossians 3:4 

Not only did Jesus conquer death for Himself, but for us. We will appear with Christ when He comes back. And we will be glorious! We must keep this hope within us.

Lastly, we must be like Paul. This good news that we have received - we cannot keep it for ourselves. We must pass on to others what we have received from God - the salvation from sin and death in Jesus Christ. For Christ did not just die for our sins but for all sins. People are heading for an eternity without their Creator. We must tell them the good news!

We must remember to preach this simple gospel to ourselves everyday. Christ came and die for our sins so that we can be made right with God. He rose so that we will rise with Him. And we need to go and share the good news. Let us take our eyes off of ourselves and keep them on Christ, for He is our only hope and salvation.

Prayers:

1)  That we may all be more deeply rooted in and transformed by the gospel.

2) That we can remember and meditate on the amazing promise that we are going to rise with Christ in His glory.

3) That we can share the gospel news with those who are lost, no matter the cost to ourselves.

writings.title: Easter Daily Devotional #2
writings.scriptureText: For rarely will someone die for a just person — though for a good person perhaps someone might even dare to die. But God proves His own love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.
writings.content:
Take heart, my soul, for it was not when I was righteous that Christ died for me, but it was when I was still a sinner. God proves His love for us in this way. For, if He had waited until we were righteous to give Christ for us, there would’ve never been a time for Christ to die for us. When we are filled with despair or shame because of our sinful nature, remember that Christ died for you while you were still a sinner. 

What a beautiful argument by Paul too. Rarely will someone die for a just person. Maybe someone would dare to die for a good person. This is true, who among us would give up our lives? Probably for a child or spouse or a very close friend. But, admittedly, the people are probably very few. People we see as just and good. But think of Christ. He knew our sinful nature and how abhorrent this is to God. And it was in this state, when we were guilty, that He went to the cross. 

What greater love is there than that? Think of it. Again, we might give up our lives for a just and good person. But for someone that we agree is immoral, never in a million years. In fact, deep down we might even find it justified for them to die. Well, remember that it was justified for us to receive eternal condemnation. And it was when we were in that state, that God sent Christ to die for us. Pitiful, unrighteousness wretches. And our shame has been turned to glory! Feeling low on God’s love today? Remember that he proved His love for you by Jesus dying when we were still sinners. 

Prayers 

1) That we may be able to know and experience God’s love for us. 

2) For a heart filled with gratitude towards Christ, since He died for us. 

3) That we may share the glorious truth of the proof of God’s love to people who do not believe using the same argument as Paul.

writings.title: Easter Daily Devotional #3
writings.scriptureText: Believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved — you and your household.
writings.content:
What must we do to be saved? There is no amount of good works, no amount of knowledge or wisdom, and no amount of striving that will save us. We simply must believe in the Lord Jesus Christ and we will be saved. 

I think this gets rather misconstrued today though. In America, people think that we just pray some prayer confessing our belief and then we are saved. This is far from the truth. For belief is more than just words. It is more than a mindset. There is action in belief. Look at the words of Jesus Himself: 

>Why do you call me, ‘Lord, Lord’, and don’t do the things I say? I will show you what someone is like who comes to me, hears my words, and acts on them. - Luke 6:46-47

If we are to call Jesus our Lord and Savior, we must do what He says. He takes it a step further and says, we hear His words and must act on them. Faith takes action as much as belief. 

And if we think deeply about this, it makes a great deal of sense. If we are to trust Jesus when he says He is, “the way, the truth, the life”, and, “nobody comes through the father accept through me”, we must also trust Him when He tells us how to live and act. We must live out our faith in action. We cannot believe only what we want to believe of what Jesus says — we must believe and obey all of it. Anything else is hypocritical. 

This is not to say we will be perfect. Far from it, but we must strive to learn how Jesus said to live and to follow His teachings. This will come with time. When we do pray that prayer, confessing our faith in Jesus, we must learn how to live out that faith. The grace of God will grow in our lives over time, and we will do more and more of what Christ says. It is easy to beat ourselves up, and think that our faith is too weak, or that we do not act on it enough. And this is what comes with time. Remember this from Romans:

>Accept the one whose faith is weak. - Romans 14:1

And also in Peter:

>But grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. 2 Peter 3:18

When we are new believer, our faith is weak and, therefore, our actions too. However, we are instructed to grow in grace and knowledge of Jesus Christ and His teachings. As we do so, our actions will speak larger and larger volume to our faith.

However, with the devotional of the day, it begs the question, where is Jesus calling you to deeper action in your faith?

Perhaps you need to gather together with fellow Christians… maybe this is a Bible study or starting to regularly attend a church, as we are all called to be part of and interact with the church (Hebrews 10:24-25). Maybe you need to go a step further, you attend church but it is time to start serving (1 Corinthians 12:12-31). Or, still yet, maybe God is calling you to start teaching and discipling others (Matthew 28:18-20). Spend some time in reflection today on where Jesus is calling you deeper. 

Prayers

1 - That we can have faith that leads to action, even when it might be detrimental to our earthly standing. 

2 - That as we obey Christ we are filled with joy and peace and that our action leads to sincere desire for holiness. 

3 - That we can feel God’s calling for us into deeper obedience and that we follow whole heartedly.

writings.title: Easter Daily Devotional #4
writings.scriptureText: For as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lords death until He comes.
writings.content:
This is a special day for this verse, as we believe the Lord’s Supper occurred on Thursday night. It’s worth asking, what is the purpose of the Lord’s Supper?

Well, first, we have the bread. The bread is the body of Christ which was broken for us. To me, this symbolizes the physical nature of Christ’s suffering for our sins. He was whipped. He was beaten. He was mocked. He was hung on a cross. And He did this for our sins. So, when we eat the bread, we must remember Christ physical agony to save us. 

And then we have the cup. The cup is Christ’s blood which was poured out for us. To me, this is the spiritual side of what Christ did for us. Jesus mentions, “This cup is the new covenant in my blood”, so Christ’s blood was the price that sealed the new covenant of grace. But why His blood? Well from Hebrews we read, 

>Indeed, under the law almost everything is purified with blood, and without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins. - ‭‭Hebrews 9:22

So, forgiveness of sins can only occur when there is shedding of blood. Why? I honestly am not sure. But Leviticus says, “For the life of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it for you on the altar to make atonement for your souls, for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life.” (Leviticus‬ ‭17‬:‭11‬) 

There is a connection between life and blood. And for sins to be atoned, an innocent party must be sacrificed to cover the sins of the guilty. A wonderful mystery indeed. And there is no greater sacrifice than the spotless Lamb of God. Let us remember today, Christ had to shed His blood for the forgiveness of our sins. There was no other way. 

And let us remember one last thing - through communion, we need to remember Christ is coming back. Let us continue to live and proclaim His death until He comes. And remember, He is coming back for you. When all seems dark, there can be nothing greater than to remember Jesus is coming back for you. 

Prayers

1 - That we be made afresh by the blood of Christ. 

2 - That whenever we participate in communion we can truly experience the grace Jesus bought. 

3 - That our hearts can rejoice as we wait expectantly for Jesus to return for us.

writings.title: Good Friday Service Script 2026
writings.scriptureText: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God and the Word was God. He was with God in the beginning. All things were created through Him and apart from Him not one thing was created that has been created. In Him was life and that life was the light of men. That light shines in the darkness, and yet the darkness did not overcome it.
writings.content:
**Opening Remarks - Avery**

Good evening everyone and welcome to the Renewal Church Good Friday service. 

My name is Avery Daniels and I will be leading the service tonight, however, there are many different faces that you will see speaking and leading worship throughout the night. 

If you have not yet been to a Good Friday service at Renewal, it is much more solemn than what we are used to for a Church service. 

Perhaps you may not know the purpose behind Good Friday. Good Friday is the day that we commemorate the crucifixion of Lord Jesus Christ. 

Maybe you are new to the faith or, if you know about the events of Good Friday, just confused as to why we call it “Good” Friday. 

We call it “Good” Friday because we live in a time where we know what happens on Sunday.

We know the salvation that Christ bought for us on the cross through His death and Sunday resurrection. 

However, if you were there that day, it would have seemed anything but good. 

That is why this service is much more solemn than what you might expect. 

For today is a day in which we seriously need to reflect on the horror of what happened to Jesus Christ. 

We must remember and honor the sacrifice that He made so that we could be made right with God.

To give a brief overview of what to expect this evening, tonight we will be focusing on the theme of darkness as Jesus prepares for, heads to, and faces the cross. 

We will have times of scripture reading, silent prayer and reflection, and worship. To end the night, we will take communion. 

Our theme verse for the night is John 1:1-5:

>In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God and the Word was God. He was with God in the beginning. All things were created through Him and apart from Him not one thing was created that has been created. In Him was life and that life was the light of men. That light shines in the darkness, and yet the darkness did not overcome it. - John 1:1-5

Now, perhaps you may be a little confused as to why this verse is the theme of the night. 

How does it relate to the crucifixion of Jesus Christ? 

From what we just read, we know that in the beginning Jesus was with God and that Jesus is God. 

We read that everything that was created, was created through Him and that in Him was life. 
His life was the light of men which shines in darkness and His light is not overcome by darkness. 

Overall, a very promising and uplifting verse. 

But, we must remember, there is darkness in which Christ’s light shines. 

That darkness is sin, our moral corruption and failures, which exists in every human being. 

While Christ’s light is not overcome by our darkness, we must remember that there is darkness within each of us. 

This is why Christ had to come and endure the horror of the cross. 

You see, the brutality of the cross is a great picture of how horrendous our sin truly is in the eyes of God. 

And while we do not like to often reflect on it, there is a healthy amount that we need to do. 

For when we realize the horror of the darkness within us, we can find light from a source that will never dim, Jesus Christ. 

As we read, reflect, and pray through the events of Jesus’s crucifixion, let us see the darkness and horror of it, and the darkness within ourselves, so that we may magnify Him all the more on Sunday. 

With that, Judith will come up to begin our journey through the progression of darkness as Jesus prepares for, heads to, and faces the cross.

**1st Scripture Analysis - Judith**

>When the hour came, he reclined at the table, and the apostles with him. Then he said to them, “I have fervently desired to eat this Passover with you before I suffer. For I tell you, I will not eat it again until it is fulfilled in the kingdom of God.” Then he took a cup, and after giving thanks, he said, “Take this and share it among yourselves. For I tell you, from now on I will not drink of the fruit of the vine until the kingdom of God comes.” And he took bread, gave thanks, broke it, gave it to them, and said, “This is my body, which is given for you. Do this in remembrance of me.” In the same way he also took the cup after supper and said, “This cup is the new covenant in my blood, which is poured out for you. But look, the hand of the one betraying me is at the table with me. For the Son of Man will go away as it has been determined, but woe to that man by whom he is betrayed! - Luke 22:14-22

Friend. Thief. Traitor. Betrayer.

These are the names we give to Judas Iscariot.

But Judas was more than a label.

He walked with Jesus. Through Jerusalem, Galilee, and Samaria.

He heard the Sermon on the Mount with his own ears.

He saw the waves obey Jesus’ command.

He watched the blind receive sight and the paralyzed rise to their feet.

He witnessed miracle after miracle.

Judas didn’t just observe the ministry. He participated in it.

He went out in Jesus’ name. He spoke to people about Him. He even healed the sick.

Judas was a disciple.

Judas was a friend.

And yet, somewhere along the way, something shifted.

He held the money bag and quietly took what wasn’t his.

What began in secret grew into something far more costly.

Small compromises became a hardened heart.

Proximity to Jesus did not mean surrender to Him.

He sat with the chief priests and asked, “What are you willing to give me if I deliver Him over to you?”

Thirty pieces of silver.

And in the garden, under the weight of that decision,

he chose a kiss as the sign of betrayal.

“Greetings, Rabbi.”

A kiss.

An act meant to show love and loyalty

twisted into the very mark of betrayal.

Surely not I, Lord?

Those words weren’t just Judas’.

They echo in all of us.

Because if we’re honest, we know the tension.

The pull between devotion and self-interest.

The quiet compromises.

The moments we choose comfort over obedience.

The moments we turn away when it matters most.

We know what it feels like to be betrayed.

The sting. The confusion. The ache.

When someone you trusted wounds you.

When someone close to you turns against you.

When the pain lingers longer than you expected.

And what do we do with that pain?

We replay the moment.

We build a case.

We justify our anger.

We want distance.

We want justice.

If we’re honest, we want revenge.

We withhold.

We harden.

We decide they are no longer worthy of our love.

And if we knew ahead of time that betrayal was coming

how would we respond?

Would we still show up?

Would we still love?

Would we still serve?

Or would we grow bitter

pull back

 protect ourselves

 and make sure we never have to feel that kind of pain again?

But Jesus.

Jesus knew.

He knew Judas would betray Him.

He knew the kiss was coming.

He knew the cross was ahead.

And still

He walked with him.

Still

He taught him.

Still

He served him.

Still

He washed his feet.

Jesus did not withhold love because betrayal was coming.

He did not harden His heart.

He did not choose self-protection over obedience.

Instead, He lived out His own teaching.

When someone takes your coat

give them your shirt also.

When someone wrongs you

love them.

When someone betrays you

extend grace.

In the garden, Jesus doesn’t run.

He doesn’t retaliate.

He doesn’t call down angels.

He steps forward.

A man of sorrows, acquainted with grief.

Despised and rejected.

Wounded for our transgressions.

Betrayed by a friend.

And still choosing the cross.

Not because He was powerless

but because He was obedient.

Because He was loving.

Because He was committed to redeeming even those who would betray Him.

And that includes us.

Because we are not just the betrayed in the story.

We are also the betrayers.

And still, Jesus steps forward.

Still, Jesus chooses the cross.

Still, Jesus offers grace.

**2nd Scripture Analysis - Lau**

>He said to them, 'I am deeply grieved to the point of death. Remain here and stay awake with me.' Going a little farther he fell facedown and prayed, 'My Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass from me. Yet not as I will, but as you will.' Then he came to the disciples and found them sleeping. He asked Peter, 'So, couldn't you stay awake with me one hour? Stay awake and pray, so that you won't enter into temptation. The spirit is willing, but the flesh is weak.' Again, a second time, he went away and prayed, 'My Father, if this cannot pass unless I drink it, your will be done.' And he came again and found them sleeping, because they could not keep their eyes open. After leaving them, he went away again and prayed a third time, saying the same thing once more. Then he came to the disciples and said to them, 'Are you still sleeping and resting? See, the time is near. The Son of Man is betrayed into the hands of sinners. Get up; let's go. See, my betrayer is near.' — Matthew 26:38–46

This scripture takes us into the final hours before Jesus' arrest, into the Garden of Gethsemane on the night of his betrayal.

Gethsemane comes from the Hebrew phrase gat shemanim, meaning "oil press." To make olive oil, olives are placed under crushing, sustained pressure until the oil is forced out. That image is not accidental. What we witness in this garden is Jesus being pressed, bearing a weight that is incomprehensible.

As he enters with Peter, James, and John, he says, "I am deeply grieved to the point of death." This is not a passing emotion. This is a grief so deep, so total, that Jesus describes it as something close to dying just from the bearing of it. He falls facedown to the ground and cries out to his Father.

And in that prayer, we see something remarkable. Both the full humanity and the full obedience of Jesus held together at once. "If it is possible, let this cup pass from me. Yet not as I will, but as you will." He is utterly honest before the Father about the weight of what is ahead, and utterly surrendered to him at the same time.

Jesus knew what this cup contained. He knew the betrayal that was already in motion. He knew the beating, the mockery, the crown of thorns. He knew the cross. And being fully human, he felt the dread of it. Not as an abstract future, but as something pressing in on him in that very moment, in that dark garden, on his knees in the dirt.

He prays this prayer not once, but three times. And each time he returns to his disciples, he finds them asleep.

In the most agonizing hours of his life, the people closest to him could not remain present with him. There is a profound loneliness in that. Not a loneliness born of abandonment exactly, but the deeper loneliness of facing something no one else could fully enter into with him. No one could truly share the weight of what Jesus was carrying that night. No one could fully understand what the next hours would require of him.

He was, in a very real sense, alone in it.

And yet he kept returning to the Father. Three times he prayed. Three times he surrendered. And when he rose from the ground the final time, he was ready. Not because the cup had been removed, but because he had placed his will fully in the Father's hands.

We see it in the very last verse, where Jesus says: "Get up; let's go. See, my betrayer is near."
There is something striking in those words. This is not a man being dragged toward the cross. This is a man who has wrestled with God in prayer, received no other answer than the one he already knew was coming, and chosen to walk forward anyway. Willingly. For us.

The darkness of Gethsemane is not a detour in the story of Easter. It is the ground it is built on. The place where Jesus, in full knowledge of the suffering ahead, said your will be done and meant it.
As we enter a time of quiet reflection, sit with the weight of what Jesus carried into that garden; His obedience to the father was because of our disobedience; And it was his love for God and for us that kept him there.

**3rd Scripture Analysis - Avery**

>At the festival the governor’s custom was to release to the crowd a prisoner they wanted. At that time they had a notorious prisoner called Barabbas. So when they had gathered together, Pilate said to them, 'Who is it you want me to release for you—Barabbas, or Jesus who is called Christ?' For he knew it was because of envy that they had handed him over. While he was sitting on the judge’s bench, his wife sent word to him, 'Have nothing to do with that righteous man, for today I’ve suffered terribly in a dream because of him.' The chief priests and the elders, however, persuaded the crowds to ask for Barabbas and to execute Jesus. The governor asked them, 'Which of the two do you want me to release for you?' 'Barabbas!' they answered. Pilate asked them, 'What should I do then with Jesus, who is called Christ?' They all answered, 'Crucify him!' Then he said, 'Why? What has he done wrong?' But they kept shouting all the more, ‘Crucify him!’ - Matthew 27:15-23

If you were there in Jerusalem that day, watching as Jesus was condemned to crucifixion, you would probably be very confused. 

This past weekend, we just celebrated Palm Sunday, where Jesus made His triumphal entry into Jerusalem, with the crowds shouting praises to the Lord Jesus. 

From Palm Sunday, to Good Friday we see a shift from shouting praise, to shouting condemnation. 
So we must ask, what changed? 

Well, I believe that nothing changed – rather, the dark, sinful nature of man is instead revealed.

What evidence was there to crucify Jesus? For what reason was he held prisoner? 

We see in this passage that Pilate himself knew that it was due to envy that the chief priests and elders had handed him over. 

So, why was Jesus a prisoner? It was due to the sinful nature of other men that He was so.
In fact, this whole passage is filled with sin. 

As I have just stated, envy, which is explicitly condoned in the Bible, was what drove the men's decisions to hand over Jesus to Pilate. 

The chief priests resented Jesus’s success and clear righteousness - so instead of trying to fix their brokenness, they decided to break someone else. 

Further, we see sin in Pilate, for he feared man more than God. 

Pilate was the authority here, and he had all the power to protect Jesus from the fate of crucifixion. 
However, he looked out at the crowd, and seeing that they might start a mob and riot, he chose to do the easy thing over the right thing. 

And finally, we have the crowd. 

The crowd is so clearly in spiritual darkness here. 

They are shouting to crucify a man whom they have just been told has done nothing wrong. 

For some, they might even be shouting to crucify a man whom they have seen do miracles and whom they were just praising less than a week before. 

Yet, the crowd is so overcome by their evil passions, they throw away all knowledge and follow the world instead of God.

However, as we reflect on this story, let us not deceive ourselves and try to clear our consciences by believing that our sin did not send Christ to the cross, but rather it was the sins of these others.

No, it was for our sins and our darkness that Christ went to the cross after being condemned to it. 

For the sake of argument, let us quickly suppose that it was not for our sins, but rather just the sins of this moment by the chief priests, Pilate, and the crowd that Jesus went to the cross. 

Listen to what the Lord says Himself in Matthew 26:53-54, 

> 'Or do you think that I cannot call on my Father, and he will provide me here and now with more than twelve legions of angels? How, then, would the Scriptures be fulfilled that say it must happen this way?' 

If Jesus was sent to the cross simply because a few people sinned, He would have called upon those angels. 

There would be no reason for Him to undergo such a vain death. 

No, my brothers and sisters, it is for our sins that Jesus was crucified. 

He went so that we could turn from darkness to light and from death to life. 

So, as the night progresses, let us not forget that it was for our sins that Jesus was crucified. 

I encourage you, throughout the remainder of the evening, to reflect on your own sinful nature that led to Christ’s enduring the sentence to death on a cross. 

And it might get dark as you reflect on your sin leading to Christ on that tree, but if Jesus is to be our light, we must first acknowledge our darkness.

**4th Scripture Analysis - Brisa**

>Then the governor’s soldiers took Jesus into the governor’s residence and gathered the whole company around him. They stripped him and dressed him in a scarlet robe. They twisted together a crown of thorns, put it on his head, and placed a staff in his right hand. And they knelt down before him and mocked him: 'Hail, king of the Jews!' Then they spat on him, took the staff, and kept hitting him on the head. After they had mocked him, they stripped him of the robe, put his own clothes on him, and led him away to crucify him. - Matthew 27:27-31

An innocent man. 

Spit on. 

Struck. 

Wounded. 

The darkness of the people’s hearts and minds enshrouded the city as they prepared to crucify Jesus. 
Before the torture that would take place on the cross, where he would be pierced for our transgressions and crushed for our iniquities, they would attack his mind and body. 

But this goes as prophesied, that though he was oppressed and afflicted, “he opened not his mouth. Like a lamb led to the slaughter.” (Isaiah 53) 

The mix of emotional and physical pain Jesus felt as he was hit and mocked by the people who once welcomed him with joy and praise – who are now making a mockery out of the holiest man who has ever lived. 

Jesus is completely humiliated - stripped of His clothes and dressed and mocked as a king. 

With their minds clouded in darkness, they mocked the King of Kings proclaiming, “Hail, King of the Jews.” 

What was covered in these hearts was wickedness, malice, and betrayal. 

Jesus was in real time experiencing the darkness of humanity. 

Humanity’s ability to torture, scourge, curse, and beat. 

Jesus’ works, love, compassion, and innocence disappeared from the minds of those who encouraged and watched in satisfaction the preparation of his crucifixion. 

There was no ounce of guilt in these moments for those afflicting, but pure physical and emotional pain of Christ. 

And in these progressing dark moments that lead to His death on a cross, he was dying for them. 

Let’s spend some quiet time and reflect on the pain Jesus experienced, and how we can appreciate the pain he bore. 

**5th Scripture Analysis - Sydney**

A large crowd of people followed him, including women who were mourning and lamenting him. But turning to them, Jesus said, 'Daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep for me, but weep for yourselves and your children. Look, the days are coming when they will say, "Blessed are the women without children, the wombs that never bore, and the breasts that never nursed!" Then they will begin to say to the mountains, "Fall on us!" and to the hills, "Cover us!" For if they do these things when the wood is green, what will happen when it is dry?' - Luke 23:27-31

Here, Jesus was being escorted to his death. He was surrounded by many who had condemned him, mocked him, and conspired against him. Surrounded by people who had called, who had begged for his crucifixion.

Every step Christ took was laden with pain. What remained of his strength was being steadily drained as he moved forward. After having been severely beaten and humiliated, he was now experiencing extreme physical exhaustion and agony en route to a death made to be a public event. 

Here, weakened from physical punishment, bombarded with verbal accusations, and walking with unquenchable thirst, Christ spoke, though he did not speak of his own challenges or his own pain. He did not argue his innocence and fight for his salvation, nor did he call for the sympathy of others in a grave display of mistreatment. He bore the weight of rejection without resistance.

Instead, Jesus urged the lamenters to turn their anguish and their mourning to themselves and those who live their life without the Lord. He turned his attention to all those to come after.

Despite such brutal treatment and incredible pain, Jesus warned of a time when the barren, who were often stigmatized, would be seen as blessed. 

Amidst Jesus’ pain, he signaled how great our need for salvation truly is. 

While his soul was sorrowful, it was not turned inward - he emphasized the sadness of a sinful state. 

The sorrow he carried was not only for suffering, but for the condition of humanity itself.
