He warned of the judgement Jerusalem faced and the mercy they’ll seek and called to question what will be done to those who lack innocence.

We can see that in a moment of intense sorrow and distress, Jesus’ attention is focused outward, not inward, and his words remain grounded in those of the Lord. 

In his pain, he continued to see others. In his darkest moments, he called attention to our own darkness. 

The moment is not his but is placed into the Lord’s divine order.

How often have we let our physical or emotional pain consume us or been tempted to feel only sorrow for ourselves? 

How often have we let it stop us from looking to the Lord? 

From living by and proclaiming his words? 

As we consider how Jesus responded to what might seem to be utter darkness, how can we reflect on times we’ve focused only on the darkness - on pain, sin, or sorrow? 

How does that impact our relationship with Christ?

Through it all, Jesus’ spirit conquered his flesh. Even in his weakest state, his trust remained unshaken. Let us let our faith dictate our circumstances, and not our circumstances dictate our faith.

**6th Scripture Reading - Sarah**

>Then two criminals were crucified with him, one on the right and one on the left. Those who passed by were yelling insults at him, shaking their heads and saying, 'You who would destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days, save yourself! If you are the Son of God, come down from the cross!' In the same way the chief priests, with the scribes and elders, mocked him and said, 'He saved others, but he cannot save himself! He is the King of Israel! Let him come down now from the cross, and we will believe in him. He trusts in God; let God rescue him now—if he takes pleasure in him! For he said, "I am the Son of God.”' In the same way even the criminals who were crucified with him taunted him. - Matthew 27:38-44

I would like to take a moment and everyone imagine a time when you made a sacrifice for someone, whether big or small. (Pause briefly) 

More than likely after you made that sacrifice you were thanked and encouraged for your actions. 

Now imagine if you had made the ultimate sacrifice not just for one person, but for every single person who would walk the face of the earth. 

Instead of being praised you are left to hang on a cross. Stripped from your clothes publicly. Nails pounded into your hands and feet. Skin shredded from whips. Thorns piercing into your head. Blood flowing from all parts of your body. Beaten, rejected, and humiliated. 

Enduring the strongest hate from the same people who used to love and follow you. The same people who lined the streets just to catch a glimpse of you… who hung on your every word… now pass by without sorrow. Now they aren’t even weeping for you while you bleed out, they are walking by and mocking you. 

Twisting the very words you had spoken and throwing them back in your face. “Save yourself.” “If you are who you say you are…”

In this moment, Jesus Christ is not only experiencing unimaginable physical pain-He is facing emotional, spiritual, and mental anguish as well. He went from having close friends and followers to absolutely no one. Left to feel completely betrayed in every form. Yet he remains there, choosing obedience to the Father and choosing love to the very people who are mocking him. Not because He has to but because He chooses to.

Reflecting more on this I am confronted with my own heart. How often do I question Jesus? Often I let my doubt, frustration, or anger shape the way I see Him and how He is working in my life. Even though it is my own actions that have led me to the place I am at. God the Father sent his son to save me from my actions. So when the doubts and questions seep in, I want to flee from those and turn back to the cross. Remembering the one who endured every form of suffering. Physical, emotional, and spiritual. Not because He had to but because He chose to. 

Jesus had the power to come down. In an instant He could have silenced every voice and saved himself. But He didn’t. He didn’t save Himself- because he was choosing to save us. 

Even in that moment, He trusted that the father was working in ways no one there could understand. Our father loves us so deeply that He made a way- even through suffering this severe-for us to have a restored and personal relationship with Him.

And I think sometimes we so quickly move past this extreme act of love and sacrifice. A gift that we are very undeserving of and fall short of daily but our Father just sits at the door waiting for us to knock. 

So I want to invite you now to take a few moments and reflect. Picturing Him on that cross- the insults and pain he is enduring. Remember that in this moment, Christ is receiving punishment for our sins. That his obedience made a way for you to have a personal relationship with God. And ask yourself- how will you respond daily to that kind of love?

**7th Scripture Analysis - David**

>And when the sixth hour had come, there was darkness over the whole land until the ninth hour. And at the ninth hour Jesus cried with a loud voice, 'Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani?' which means, 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' And some of the bystanders hearing it said, 'Behold, he is calling Elijah.' And someone ran and filled a sponge with sour wine, put it on a reed and gave it to him to drink, saying, 'Wait, let us see whether Elijah will come to take him down.' And Jesus uttered a loud cry and breathed his last. And the curtain of the temple was torn in two, from top to bottom. And when the centurion, who stood facing him, saw that in this way he breathed his last, he said, 'Truly this man was the Son of God!' - Mark 15:33–39


Tonight, we examine one of the darkest moments in all of Scripture, and humanity.

In Gospel of Mark chapter 15, we’re told that from noon until three in the afternoon, darkness covered the whole land.

No ordinary shadow, not just clouds, but a deep, unsettling darkness.

This isn’t just a detail about the weather.

This is God’s creation responding.

Throughout the Old Testament, darkness is often a sign of judgment, a sign that something holy and heavy is taking place.

And here, at the cross, we see that weight fall fully on Jesus.

The Light of the world

Surrounded by darkness.

And then, Jesus cries out—

“My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” quoting Psalm 22, a psalm that holds great anguish.
However this is not simply a reference. This is real.

Jesus is not simply pointing to Scripture—He is living it, and feeling it.

In this moment, He is experiencing the full weight of sin

The full depth of separation from God

The reality of what it means to be forsaken.

And we have to understand—this wasn’t easy to say.

On the cross, even breathing is painful.

Jesus would’ve had to push Himself up against the nails in His hands and feet… just to get enough air, let alone speak. Every word cost Him something.

What kind of agony must that have been?

Then, He breathes His last.

And something incredible happens.

The curtain in the temple is torn in two—from top to bottom.

The barrier between God and humanity… is gone.

And standing there, watching all of this unfold, is a Roman centurion—an outsider, someone far from God.

And he says,

“Surely this man was the Son of God.”

He could truly see Jesus, not in His miracles, or His teachings,

But in His suffering and death.

In the darkness of what had just transpired before him.

And that’s what Good Friday invites us into.

To sit in the darkness.

To feel the weight of it.

To recognize that Jesus didn’t avoid it

He stepped into it.

And tonight, we don’t rush past this.

We don’t skip ahead.

We sit here,

at the cross,

in the darkness,

and we remember what Jesus endured.

**Communion - Avery**

Before our closing remarks, we will go into communion for the evening. 

As we read tonight, the first communion took place the night before Good Friday. 

1 Corinthians 11:23-26 gives us a great picture of communion:

>For I received from the Lord what I also passed on to you: On the night when he was betrayed, the Lord Jesus took bread, and when he had given thanks, broke it, and said, 'This is my body, which is for you. Do this in remembrance of me.' In the same way also he took the cup, after supper, and said, 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood. Do this, as often as you drink it, in remembrance of me.' For as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord’s death until he comes. 

As we take the bread and the cup, let us remember what we discussed this evening. 

As we break the bread, we remember the body of Christ, stripped, beaten, hung on the tree, given for you. So now, take the bread, which is Christ’s body given for us on that cross. 

Let us now take the cup, which is the new covenant, bought for us by the precious blood of the Son of God, Jesus Christ. Drink this cup in remembrance of Him.

I want to point us to an important reminder from the Apostle Paul about communion from 1 Corinthians 11:27, 

>So, then, whoever eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner will be guilty of sin against the body and blood of the Lord.

We must remember the honor that is due Christ for His sacrifice when we take communion. 

Let us not eat the bread or drink the cup in an unworthy manner. 

We have reflected on the horror of the cross and our sinful nature tonight. 

Let us confess that nature to God now - any unconfessed sin or dullness of spirit let us bring it before the LORD. 

Let us remember Christ, who endured such darkness for us. 

Join me in a time of silent prayer and confession to God. Be honest with Him - not just about being a sinner, but how you have sinned.

**Closing Remarks - Avery**

As we close for the evening, I want to remind you of where we left off in the Good Friday story. 

At this moment, the darkness seems to have won, as our Lord hangs dead on the cross. 

However, I don’t want to leave us without hope for the night. 

Yes, the cross was dark. 

Yes, the cross was brutal. 

Yes, the cross was terrible. 

Yes, this horror reflects the horror of sin within us, which you might’ve even felt during your time of reflection or confession. 

These are all things we need to remember, reflect, and meditate on. 

However, Jesus Christ endured the cross willingly, for He Himself said, 

>This is why the Father loves me, because I lay down my life so that I may take it up again. No one takes it from me, but I lay it down on my own. - John 10:17-18

But why did He choose to give His life for us? 

Well, let me recall your attention to the scripture we read about tonight in the gospel according to Matthew, where the thieves on the cross were taunting Jesus. Now, let us read this story in the gospel according to Luke:

>Then one of the criminals hanging there began to yell insults at him: 'Aren’t you the Messiah? Save yourself and us!' But the other answered, rebuking him: 'Don’t you even fear God, since you are undergoing the same punishment? We are punished justly, because we’re getting back what we deserve for the things we did, but this man has done nothing wrong.' Then he said, 'Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom.' And he said to him, 'Truly I tell you, today you will be with me in paradise.' - Luke 23:39-43

You see, we can be like either thief.

At first, both the thieves are mocking and taunting Jesus to save them even though they are punished justly for their actions. 

They try to manipulate Jesus to get what they want as we all can so often do. 

It is only in surrender to Jesus that we are rescued. 

The thief who repents and turns to Christ for forgiveness and salvation is the perfect representation of the gospel. 

We, who would be punished justly for our sins by eternal damnation, are able to enter the Kingdom of Christ if we would repent and turn to him. 

I’ll ask then again, why did Christ choose to give His life for us?

It’s so that we too can turn from our lives of mocking God, to receive new life through Christ being made right with God.

So yes, our Savior hanging dead on the cross is a dark moment, but remember, His light shines in the darkness and the darkness did not overcome it. 

His words to the thief are the same to you and me, if we would have him. 

We will be with Him in paradise. 

That glimmer of light remains, and will be fully realized on Sunday morning.

And, lastly, I would like to say one more thing. 

Perhaps you sit here and you cannot get past the horror of sin in yourself. 

I know it - I’ve been there too, and admittedly, still can be. 

If this is you, perhaps you would say to me, "sir, it is dark within me. 

And each day it seems to get darker still."

You might think, “I believe that there is Salvation in Jesus Christ, but I am not sure I believe it is for me.” 

You might continue on and think, “this darkness within and around me is too dark, there is no way Jesus could take me. So much darkness has surrounded me, I do not think God can see or rescue me from my darkness.”

To that, I would say, let us remember these words of David from Psalm 139:11-12 as we reflect on our darkness: 

>If I say, ‘Surely the darkness will hide me, and the light around me will be night’— even the darkness is not dark to you. The night shines like the day; darkness and light are alike to you.

So, if you believe that your darkness is too dark, I would say to you - God sees us in our light and in our darkness. 

So yes, let us remember the darkness in us. 

Let it bring us to our knees. 

But no, let it never bring us to despair. 

For God sees you even through the darkness. 

And I promise you, come Sunday morning, He will save you and make you anew. 

That light of Christ shines in the darkness, and yet the darkness did not overcome it. 

I pray that light will shine wholly in each of us, for Christ’s sake, Amen.
